key_insight: Plasma-sprayed In625 on T22 can resist early nitrate-salt exposure at 500 °C, but prolonged exposure leads to coating degradation and corrosion penetration toward the substrate.
engineering_relevance: For CSP thermal-energy-storage hardware, this coating offers a protective option for carbon steel components, but lifetime under molten nitrate salts must be carefully managed.
method: Deposited Inconel 625 on T22 carbon steel by Compact Plasma Spray.
Exposed coated samples to eutectic nitrate salt at 500 °C for 24, 72, 168, 336, and 504 h.
Assessed corrosion progression from surface film formation to through-thickness attack.
evidence_signal: After 24 h, corrosion products were limited to a thin surface layer, whereas longer exposures caused degradation to spread through the coating and reach the coating/substrate interface by about 500 h.
watch_item: The sprayed splat structure had low cohesion, contributing to a relatively high corrosion rate and suggesting that coating microstructure control is critical for real CSP service.
paper_title: Corrosion Behavior of Plasma-Sprayed In625 Coatings Exposed to Nitrate Salts in Concentrating Solar Power Plants
